text: How many people are on your team?
I don't have any time on Friday.
Paula, hi, it's Greg. Thanks for the two lines. I've got two minutes before my next call, so I'll be quick, what's the deductible on the factory account?
Sorry, Greg. Could you speak more slowly?
Of course. The deductible. On the factory account. How much is it?
One word, what does deductible mean?
Good question. It's the part the client pays before the insurance pays. In Portuguese, franquia.
Ah, franquia. Just a moment, let me check the file.
The deductible is fifty thousand. Five-zero.
Five-zero. Fifty thousand. Sorry, the line is bad, did you catch that?
I caught it. Fifty. Perfect, Paula, and you just did that whole call in English without stopping once. That's the job.

Hi, Paula. David here. You have a call with Greg this week, and I know exactly what you are afraid of. You are afraid he says something fast, you do not catch it, and he finds out your English is not perfect. So let me give you the one thing nobody gives you. Ready? He already knows your English is not perfect. He does not care. And here is the part you will not believe: I am American, I was born with this language, and I ask people to repeat things every single day. On the phone, in meetings, with my own mother. Sorry, could you say that again? What does that mean? One moment, let me check. These are not beginner phrases, Paula. These are the phrases everybody uses, in every language, all day. The person who does not understand and asks is fine. The person who does not understand and stays silent, and nods, and guesses the number wrong, that person is the problem. When you do not catch something on that call, do not freeze, and do not go back to Portuguese. Just ask. Could you repeat that? Four words. They keep the whole call alive, and nobody, nobody, thinks less of you for saying them. The only bad move is guessing. See you after the call.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. One thing before your call, and it is not about English. I have watched this company for twenty years, and I want to tell you who wins here. It is not the person with the best English. It is the person who asks. When a broker gives me a word I do not know, I stop him. What does that mean? When a client says a number too fast, I say it back, and I ask him to repeat. Every time. The people who ask are the professionals, Paula. The ones who nod and smile and understood nothing, those are the ones I worry about, because in our business a wrong word is not an embarrassment, it is somebody's factory, it is money. So when you are on that call with Greg and something goes past you, do exactly what I do. Say, sorry, could you repeat that? Ask what the word means. Take a moment. Nobody at this company has ever lost an account for asking a question. Not one. People lose accounts for guessing, for nodding, for being too proud to say I did not catch that. You are not going into that call to be perfect. You are going in to understand. And understanding starts with asking. See you Monday.
Just a moment, let me check.
